perturbation.original_text: 7. Sales and Other Taxes. The Company will add sales, excise and any other tax or surcharge to its invoices which it is obligated to collect and remit under the laws of the State of Texas, the United States or any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Sales and Other Taxes. The Company will add sales, excise, and any other tax or surcharge to its invoices which it deems necessary to collect and remit, regardless of legal obligation under the laws of the State of Texas, the United States or any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the collection of taxes discretionary, contradicting the legal requirement to collect and remit applicable taxes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Tax Code § 151.004 - Responsibility for Tax
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Tax Code § 151.004 clearly states the responsibility for collecting and remitting sales taxes lies with the seller. The modified text allows the Company to decide which taxes to collect, contradicting this legal obligation.

"Internet" means collectively the myriad of computer and telecommunications facilities, including equipment and operating software, that comprise the interconnected worldwide network of networks that employ the Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol, or any predecessor or successor protocols to the protocol, to communicate information of all kinds by wire or radio. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 394, Sec. 1, eff. Oct. 1, 1999. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. (a) "Internet access service" means a service that enables users to access content, information, electronic mail, or other services offered over the Internet and may also include access to proprietary content, information, and other services as part of a package of services offered to consumers. The term does not include telecommunications services. (b) "Internet access service" does not include and the exemption under Section 151.325 does not apply to any other taxable service listed in Section 151.0101 (a), unless the taxable service is provided in conjunction with and is merely incidental to the provision of Internet access service. (c) On and after October 1, 1999, "Internet access service" is not included in the definitions of "data processing service" and "information service." perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Company to collect taxes only when it 'deems necessary,' explicitly stating 'regardless of legal obligation.' This directly contradicts the original text, which mandates collection and remittance based on legal obligations. The Law Explanation references Texas Tax Code § 151.004, highlighting the seller's responsibility for collecting and remitting sales taxes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 'Sec. 151.004. 'IN THIS STATE'. 'In this state' means within the exterior limits of Texas,' while defining 'in this state', does not directly contradict the modified text but gives a definition that when coupled with the law explanation, highlights how the changed text is a contradiction of the law.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Force Majeure. In the event that either party shall be prevented from performing any of its obligations due under the terms of this Agreement by an act of God, by acts of war, riot, or civil commotion, by an act of State, by strikes, fire, flood, or by the occurrence of any other event beyond the control of the parties hereto, that party shall be excused from any further performance of the obligations and undertakings set forth under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Force Majeure. In the event that either party is slightly inconvenienced in performing any of its obligations due under the terms of this Agreement due to weather, minor illness, or slight economic downturn, that party shall be excused from any further performance of the obligations and undertakings set forth under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text broadens the definition of 'force majeure' to include minor inconveniences, deviating significantly from the legally understood scope of events that constitute force majeure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Business and Commerce Code § 2.615 - Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2.615 of the Texas Business and Commerce Code, which relates to the Uniform Commercial Code, addresses circumstances excusing performance due to unforeseen events fundamentally altering the contract's nature. The modification here includes circumstances that do not fundamentally alter the agreement, thus improperly expanding what constitutes a legally recognized 'force majeure' event.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ty reinstates the contract if he accepts a non-conforming installment without seasonably notifying of cancellation or if he brings an action with respect only to past installments or demands performance as to future installments. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.613. CASUALTY TO IDENTIFIED GOODS. Where the contract requires for its performance goods identified when the contract is made, and the goods suffer casualty without fault of either party before the risk of loss passes to the buyer, or in a proper case under a "no arrival, no sale" term (Section 2.324 ) then (1) if the loss is total the contract is avoided; and (2) if the loss is partial or the goods have so deteriorated as no longer to conform to the contract the buyer may nevertheless demand inspection and at his option either treat the contract as avoided or accept the goods with due allowance from the contract price for the deterioration or the deficiency in quantity but without further right against the seller. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.614. SUBSTITUTED PERFORMANCE. (a) Where without fault of either party the agreed berthing, loading, or unloading facilities fail or an agreed type of carrier becomes unavailable or the agreed manner of delivery otherwise becomes commercially impracticable but a commercially reasonable substitute is available, such substitute performance must be tendered and accepted. (b) If the agreed means or manner of payment fails because of domestic or foreign governmental regulation, the seller may withhold or stop delivery unless the buyer provides a means or manner of payment which is commercially a substantial equivalent. If delivery has already been taken, payment by the means or in the manner provided by the regulation discharges the buyer's obligation unless the regulation is discriminatory, oppressive or predatory. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.615. EXCUSE BY FAILURE OF PRESUPPOSED CONDITIONS. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (1) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with Subdivisions (2) and (3) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (2) Where the causes mentioned in Subdivision (1) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (3) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under Subdivision (2), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.616. PROCEDURE ON NOTICE CLAIMING EXCUSE. (a) Where the buyer receives notification of a material or indefinite delay or an allocation justified under the preceding section he may by written notification to the seller as to any delivery concerned, and where the prospective deficiency substantially impairs the value of the whole contract under the provisions of this chapter relating to breach of installment contracts (Section 2.612 ), then also as to the whole, (1) terminate and thereby discharge any unexecuted portion of the contract; or (2) modify the contract by agreeing to take his available quota in substitution. (b) If after receipt of such notification from the seller the buyer fails
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the legal understanding of 'force majeure'. The original text includes events like 'acts of God, war, riot,' which are beyond the parties' control. The changed text broadens this to include 'weather, minor illness, or slight economic downturn'. This is a misalignment of terminology. The Law Explanation references Section 2.615 of the Texas Business and Commerce Code, stating it addresses circumstances excusing performance due to unforeseen events fundamentally altering the contract's nature. Scraped Law Snippet 1 also includes the text: 'Excuse by failure of presupposed conditions'. The modified text includes circumstances that do not fundamentally alter the agreement, thus improperly expanding what constitutes a legally recognized 'force majeure' event, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Exculpatory Clause. The parties agree that Cano will not be liable to the Company or any third party for any injury sustained by the Company, its employees, independent contractors, invitees or any other third party while preparing for, filming or working on post production of any episode.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Exculpatory Clause. The parties agree that Cano will not be liable to the Company or any third party for any injury, including those resulting from gross negligence or willful misconduct, sustained by the Company, its employees, independent contractors, invitees or any other third party while preparing for, filming or working on post production of any episode.
perturbation.explanation: This attempts to exculpate Cano from liability even for gross negligence or willful misconduct. Such broad exculpatory clauses are often unenforceable under Texas law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas law regarding exculpatory agreements and gross negligence
perturbation.law_citation: TX - Nabors Drilling USA, LP v. Patterson, 517 S.W.3d 81, 92 (Tex. 2017)
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law, as interpreted in *Nabors Drilling USA, LP v. Patterson*, holds that exculpatory clauses cannot shield a party from liability for its own gross negligence. This modification seeks to do exactly that, rendering the clause likely unenforceable and contradicting established legal principles.
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to exculpate Cano from liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct. The Law Explanation says that, according to Texas law, exculpatory clauses cannot shield a party from liability for its own gross negligence. This directly contradicts established legal principles, making the clause unenforceable.
